questions.stem: An 86-year-old woman with CKD stage 3, atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and heart failure takes: warfarin, metformin, lisinopril, furosemide, spironolactone, digoxin, and glipizide. She is admitted with dizziness, yellow-green visual halos, nausea, and bradycardia (HR 44, irregular). INR is 5.1 and potassium is 3.0 mEq/L. Which drug-drug-disease interaction cascade best explains her presentation?

options.text: Metformin toxicity causing lactic acidosis, with glipizide-induced hypoglycemia masking neurological symptoms
Supratherapeutic warfarin (INR 5.1) causing a bleeding stroke, presenting as visual disturbances and bradycardia
Digoxin toxicity (yellow-green visual halos, bradycardia, nausea) precipitated by furosemide-induced hypokalemia, compounded by reduced renal clearance from CKD stage 3
Lisinopril-induced hyperkalemia causing bradycardia, with warfarin causing visual disturbances through retinal bleeding

questions.rationale: The clinical picture is classic digoxin toxicity: the pathognomonic triad of GI symptoms (nausea, anorexia), cardiac effects (bradycardia, heart block, dysrhythmias), and visual changes (yellow-green halos, blurred vision, scotomas). Precipitating cascade: (1) Furosemide → hypokalemia (K+ 3.0 mEq/L); (2) Spironolactone partially counteracts furosemide-induced K+ loss but is insufficient; (3) Hypokalemia increases digoxin-Na+/K+-ATPase binding affinity → potentiates digoxin effect even at previously therapeutic serum levels; (4) CKD stage 3 reduces renal digoxin clearance → drug accumulation; (5) Supratherapeutic INR (5.1) is a separate finding — likely from dietary change or drug interaction affecting warfarin metabolism. Management: hold digoxin, carefully replete potassium (IV in symptomatic bradycardia), cardiac monitoring, Digibind (digoxin immune Fab) for severe bradycardia or life-threatening dysrhythmia; hold warfarin and administer vitamin K for INR reversal.

distractorRationales.a: Metformin toxicity causing lactic acidosis, with glipizide-induced hypoglycemia masking neurological symptoms misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology warfarin points to Digoxin toxicity (yellow-green visual halos, bradycardia, nausea) precipitated by furosemide-induced hypokalemia, compounded by reduced renal clearance, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.b: Supratherapeutic warfarin (INR 5.1) causing a bleeding stroke, presenting as visual disturbances and bradycardia misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology warfarin points to Digoxin toxicity (yellow-green visual halos, bradycardia, nausea) precipitated by furosemide-induced hypokalemia, compounded by reduced renal clearance, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Lisinopril-induced hyperkalemia causing bradycardia, with warfarin causing visual disturbances through retinal bleeding misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology warfarin points to Digoxin toxicity (yellow-green visual halos, bradycardia, nausea) precipitated by furosemide-induced hypokalemia, compounded by reduced renal clearance, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Digoxin toxicity: yellow-green halos + bradycardia + nausea. Hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity even at 'therapeutic' levels. CKD reduces digoxin clearance. Treat: hold digoxin, correct K+, Digibind for severe cases.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse discovers that a colleague has been diverting controlled substances — taking opioids from the medication dispensing cabinet that are charted as wasted but are not being wasted appropriately. The colleague appears to be under the influence during a shift. Which action is the nurse's ethical and legal obligation?

options.text: Speak privately with the colleague and give them a chance to self-report before taking action
Document the suspicion in a personal diary for future reference if the behavior continues
Report immediately to the charge nurse and nursing supervisor, following facility policy for suspected substance diversion
Contact the State Board of Nursing directly to file a formal complaint without involving hospital administration

questions.rationale: Suspected drug diversion by a healthcare colleague must be reported immediately through the chain of command — charge nurse and supervisor — per facility policy. This protects patient safety (a potentially impaired nurse poses immediate risk), fulfills the nurse's ethical obligation under the ANA Code of Ethics, and triggers the appropriate investigation process. Giving the colleague time to self-report (A) delays patient safety action and may allow evidence destruction. Personal documentation (B) does not fulfill the reporting obligation. Going directly to the State BON (D) bypasses the facility process, which should occur first — BON involvement follows internal reporting.

distractorRationales.a: Speak privately with the colleague and give them a chance to self-report before taking action misses the clinical pattern: Mandatory Reporting safety points to Report immediately to the charge nurse and nursing supervisor, following facility policy for suspected substance diversion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.b: Document the suspicion in a personal diary for future reference if the behavior continues misses the clinical pattern: Mandatory Reporting safety points to Report immediately to the charge nurse and nursing supervisor, following facility policy for suspected substance diversion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Contact the State Board of Nursing directly to file a formal complaint without involving hospital administration misses the clinical pattern: Mandatory Reporting safety points to Report immediately to the charge nurse and nursing supervisor, following facility policy for suspected substance diversion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Suspected drug diversion: report immediately to charge nurse and supervisor per facility policy — patient safety is the priority; ethical obligation requires reporting, not covering up.

questions.stem: A neonate is assessed at 1 minute of life. The nurse notes: heart rate 98 bpm, weak cry, some flexion of extremities, grimace only in response to stimulation, and blue hands and feet with pink body. What is the APGAR score?
questions.rationale: APGAR scoring: Appearance (color): pink body, blue hands/feet (acrocyanosis) = 1. Pulse (HR): 98 bpm (<100) = 1. Grimace (reflex): grimace only = 1. Activity (muscle tone): some flexion = 1. Respirations: weak cry = 1. Total APGAR = 5. Score 7–10 = normal. Score 4–6 = moderate depression — requires oxygen and stimulation. Score 0–3 = severe depression requiring immediate resuscitation.

distractorRationales.b: 6 misses the clinical pattern: Neonatal APGAR oxygen points to 5, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: 7 misses the clinical pattern: Neonatal APGAR oxygen points to 5, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: 8 misses the clinical pattern: Neonatal APGAR oxygen points to 5, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: APGAR ≤6 = requires intervention; acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet only) = 1 point; central cyanosis (blue trunk/face) = 0 points.

questions.stem: A patient develops respiratory distress 12 hours after total thyroidectomy, with stridor, dysphagia, and progressive anterior neck swelling. SpO2 is 88% on 6 L/min nasal cannula. Which action is the NURSE'S IMMEDIATE PRIORITY?

options.text: Administer calcium gluconate IV and draw ionized calcium level
Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression
Increase oxygen to 15 L/min via non-rebreather and call rapid response
Position the patient in high Fowler's and prepare for emergent intubation

questions.rationale: Post-thyroidectomy respiratory distress with anterior neck swelling indicates expanding hematoma compressing the trachea. This is an airway emergency. Bedside wound opening to evacuate the hematoma is the immediate intervention — wound opening kits should be at the bedside on all post-thyroidectomy patients for this reason. While this is being done, the team prepares for emergent surgical re-exploration. Calcium gluconate (A) treats hypocalcemia from hypoparathyroidism (tetany, perioral numbness, Trousseau's sign) — not the presenting emergency. NRB mask (C) won't overcome a mechanical airway obstruction.

distractorRationales.a: Administer calcium gluconate IV and draw ionized calcium level misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced airway points to Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Increase oxygen to 15 L/min via non-rebreather and call rapid response misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced airway points to Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Position the patient in high Fowler's and prepare for emergent intubation misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced airway points to Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Post-thyroidectomy neck swelling + stridor = hematoma compressing airway → open the surgical wound immediately. Keep wound kit at bedside.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient newly prescribed lithium about safety monitoring. Which statement by the patient indicates correct understanding of lithium toxicity prevention?

options.text: 'I should limit salt in my diet to prevent water retention since lithium can cause edema.'
'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea, vomiting, or excessive sweating.'
'I can stop taking lithium when I feel better, as long as my mood is stable.'
'Ibuprofen is safe to take for headaches while on lithium.'

questions.rationale: Lithium is excreted by the kidney and competes with sodium for reabsorption. When sodium levels drop (from low-sodium diet, diarrhea, vomiting, sweating, diuretics), the kidney retains lithium — causing toxicity. Consistent sodium and fluid intake, and monitoring for dehydration causes, are essential safety measures. Low-sodium diet (A) increases lithium retention and toxicity risk — the opposite of safe. Abrupt discontinuation (C) causes rebound mania. NSAIDs like ibuprofen (D) decrease renal lithium excretion, raising lithium levels significantly — they are contraindicated.

distractorRationales.a: 'I should limit salt in my diet to prevent water retention since lithium can cause edema.' misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity dehydration points to 'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.c: 'I can stop taking lithium when I feel better, as long as my mood is stable.' misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity dehydration points to 'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: 'Ibuprofen is safe to take for headaches while on lithium.' misses the clinical pattern: Lithium Toxicity dehydration points to 'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Lithium safety: maintain consistent sodium/fluid intake; avoid NSAIDs (raise lithium levels); report vomiting/diarrhea/excessive sweating immediately.

questions.stem: A nurse is with a patient who suddenly begins experiencing a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. What is the PRIORITY nursing action during the seizure?

options.text: Insert an oral airway to prevent tongue biting and aspiration
Restrain the patient's limbs to prevent injury
Protect the patient's head, clear the environment of hazards, turn the patient on their side, and time the seizure
Administer diazepam 10 mg IV immediately per standing orders

questions.rationale: During an active seizure, the nursing priorities are safety and observation: protect the head (soft padding), clear hazardous objects from the immediate environment, position on the side (lateral decubitus) to prevent aspiration when the seizure subsides, and time the duration. Nothing should be inserted into the mouth during an active seizure — this can cause injury to the patient and the nurse, and the jaw cannot be pried open safely. Physical restraint can cause musculoskeletal injury during tonic-clonic movements. Medication (benzodiazepine) is indicated for prolonged seizures (> 5 minutes, status epilepticus) — not every seizure automatically requires IV medication.

distractorRationales.a: Insert an oral airway to prevent tongue biting and aspiration misses the clinical pattern: Neurological / Seizure Management aspiration points to Protect the patient's head, clear the environment of hazards, turn the patient on their side, and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.b: Restrain the patient's limbs to prevent injury misses the clinical pattern: Neurological / Seizure Management aspiration points to Protect the patient's head, clear the environment of hazards, turn the patient on their side, and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Administer diazepam 10 mg IV immediately per standing orders misses the clinical pattern: Neurological / Seizure Management aspiration points to Protect the patient's head, clear the environment of hazards, turn the patient on their side, and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Active seizure: PROTECT (head, environment, airway position) → TIME → DO NOT insert objects in mouth, DO NOT restrain limbs. Post-seizure: lateral recovery position, assess airway, vitals, neuro. Administer benzodiazepines for status epilepticus (> 5 minutes).

visualRationale.title: Neurological / Seizure Management
visualRationale.caption: Active seizure: PROTECT (head, environment, airway position) → TIME → DO NOT insert objects in mouth, DO NOT restrain limbs. Post-seizure: lateral recovery position, assess airway, vitals, neuro. Administer benzodiazepines for status epilepticus (> 5 minutes).
visualRationale.conclusion: Active seizure: PROTECT (head, environment, airway position) → TIME → DO NOT insert objects in mouth, DO NOT restrain limbs. Post-seizure: lateral recovery position, assess airway, vitals, neuro. Administer benzodiazepines for status epilepticus (> 5 minutes).

questions.stem: A parent asks the nurse why their 8-year-old child cannot be given aspirin for a fever during this influenza illness. The nurse's teaching should be based on which evidence-based concern?

options.text: Aspirin causes gastrointestinal bleeding in children under 12
Aspirin use in children with viral illness (especially influenza and varicella) is associated with Reye's syndrome — a rare but potentially fatal encephalopathy and hepatotoxicity
Aspirin causes febrile seizures in school-age children
Aspirin inhibits platelet function and increases bleeding risk in children with viral infections

questions.rationale: Reye's syndrome is a rare but life-threatening condition characterized by non-inflammatory encephalopathy and microvesicular hepatic steatosis. It is strongly associated with aspirin use in children during viral illnesses, particularly influenza (types A and B) and varicella. Pathophysiology: salicylate-induced mitochondrial dysfunction during viral illness. Presentation: viral prodrome → vomiting → altered mental status → cerebral edema → death (mortality 20–40%). Since the CDC advisory in the 1980s recommending against aspirin in children <18 during viral illnesses, Reye's syndrome incidence has fallen dramatically. Preferred antipyretics: acetaminophen or ibuprofen.

distractorRationales.a: Aspirin causes gastrointestinal bleeding in children under 12 misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing edema points to Aspirin use in children with viral illness (especially influenza and varicella) is associated with Reye's syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Aspirin causes febrile seizures in school-age children misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing edema points to Aspirin use in children with viral illness (especially influenza and varicella) is associated with Reye's syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Aspirin inhibits platelet function and increases bleeding risk in children with viral infections misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing edema points to Aspirin use in children with viral illness (especially influenza and varicella) is associated with Reye's syndrome, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Reye's syndrome: aspirin + viral illness (influenza, varicella) in children → encephalopathy + hepatotoxicity; never give aspirin to children <18 with viral illness; use acetaminophen or ibuprofen.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Reye's syndrome: aspirin + viral illness (influenza, varicella) in children → encephalopathy + hepatotoxicity; never give aspirin to children <18 with viral illness; use acetaminophen or ibuprofen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Reye's syndrome: aspirin + viral illness (influenza, varicella) in children → encephalopathy + hepatotoxicity; never give aspirin to children <18 with viral illness; use acetaminophen or ibuprofen.

questions.stem: A patient in the PACU states she was awake during her surgery — she recalls the surgeons' conversation, felt pressure on her abdomen but no pain, and could not move or signal anyone. She is trembling, tearful, and says 'It was the most terrifying experience of my life.' Which nursing response and plan of care is most appropriate?

options.text: Reassure her that awareness under anesthesia is medically impossible with modern monitoring, and that she was likely dreaming

Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per policy, provide immediate emotional support, and initiate referral for trauma-focused psychological counseling for potential PTSD
Document 'patient reports dream during anesthesia' and notify only the charge nurse
Administer a PRN anxiolytic and reassess her emotional state in 2 hours before taking further action

questions.rationale: Intraoperative awareness with recall (accidental awareness during general anesthesia — AAGA) affects approximately 1-2 per 1,000 general anesthetics. Patients may recall sounds, conversation, touch, pressure, or in severe cases pain and complete paralysis (most traumatizing — the patient is conscious but paralyzed and unable to alert the team). PTSD develops in up to 70% of awareness cases if not addressed. Nursing priorities: (1) BELIEVE the patient — dismissing her account (gaslighting) causes profound additional psychological harm, violates patient dignity, and creates significant legal liability; (2) Validate her distress and provide immediate compassionate support; (3) Document her exact words accurately and objectively; (4) Notify the anesthesia provider — awareness should trigger review of anesthetic depth monitoring (BIS), neuromuscular blockade management, and volatile agent delivery; (5) Notify risk management per institutional policy; (6) Refer promptly for trauma-focused therapy (EMDR or CBT for PTSD). Administering sedatives to suppress the patient's report is not appropriate.

distractorRationales.a: Reassure her that awareness under anesthesia is medically impossible with modern monitoring, and that she was misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care pain points to Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Document 'patient reports dream during anesthesia' and notify only the charge nurse misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care pain points to Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Administer a PRN anxiolytic and reassess her emotional state in 2 hours before taking further action misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care pain points to Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Intraoperative awareness: BELIEVE and VALIDATE the patient. Document accurately. Notify anesthesia + risk management. Refer for trauma/PTSD counseling. Dismissing the experience causes psychological harm. Affects ~1-2/1,000 GAs.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient receiving a blood transfusion who develops hives, pruritus, and a temperature of 38.5°C (101.3°F) 20 minutes into the transfusion. What is the FIRST nursing action?

options.text: Slow the transfusion rate and administer diphenhydramine as ordered
Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and blood bank
Complete the transfusion quickly since the reaction appears mild
Obtain a blood culture and administer acetaminophen for the fever

questions.rationale: Any signs of a transfusion reaction—including hives, pruritus, and fever—require immediate cessation of the transfusion. The key concern is that mild symptoms can rapidly progress to severe hemolytic or anaphylactic reactions. The tubing must be disconnected (not just slowed) and the IV kept patent with normal saline using new tubing to prevent infusion of remaining blood product. The provider and blood bank must be notified, and the blood bag and tubing are returned to the blood bank for analysis. Continuing or completing the transfusion risks life-threatening escalation.

distractorRationales.a: Slow the transfusion rate and administer diphenhydramine as ordered misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Complete the transfusion quickly since the reaction appears mild misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Obtain a blood culture and administer acetaminophen for the fever misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Transfusion reaction protocol: STOP → Saline via new line → Notify provider + blood bank → Return blood bag + tubing → Document. Never slow the rate and continue if a reaction is suspected.

questions.stem: A public health nurse is performing TB contact tracing after identifying an active pulmonary TB case in an elementary school teacher. The school has 420 students and 35 staff. Which group should be prioritized for PPD/IGRA testing and evaluated first?

options.text: All community residents within a 1-mile radius of the school
Close contacts — students in the infected teacher's classroom and staff sharing the teachers' lounge (highest-risk exposure contacts)
Only the principal and vice-principal as the administrative contacts
All 420 students simultaneously to ensure no child is missed

questions.rationale: TB contact investigation follows a concentric circle approach. Priority 1 — close contacts (highest risk): household members, individuals with prolonged face-to-face exposure in an enclosed space. For a school teacher: students in the infected teacher's classroom (daily prolonged contact), colleagues sharing enclosed spaces (teachers' lounge, planning periods). Priority 2 — casual contacts: students in the same school but different classrooms. Priority 3 — community contacts. All close contacts receive TST/IGRA regardless of symptom status. Positive contacts → chest X-ray → LTBI vs active TB determination → treatment. Mass testing of all 420 students before stratifying risk wastes resources and creates unnecessary anxiety.

distractorRationales.a: All community residents within a 1-mile radius of the school misses the clinical pattern: Community Health pulmonary points to Close contacts — students in the infected teacher's classroom and staff sharing the teachers' lounge (highest-risk, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Only the principal and vice-principal as the administrative contacts misses the clinical pattern: Community Health pulmonary points to Close contacts — students in the infected teacher's classroom and staff sharing the teachers' lounge (highest-risk, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: All 420 students simultaneously to ensure no child is missed misses the clinical pattern: Community Health pulmonary points to Close contacts — students in the infected teacher's classroom and staff sharing the teachers' lounge (highest-risk, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: TB contact tracing: concentric circle approach — prioritize closest contacts first (classroom students, shared-space colleagues); cascade outward only if high conversion rate found in inner circle.
